title: The Grid Router: Mission 5's Full Routing and Contingency System
concept: Final Grid Routing and Contingency Module (capstone synthesis)
learningObjectives: Combine topology modeling, shortest-path/flow routing, resilience analysis, and RL-augmented candidate selection into one integrated routing system
Answer the mission's two founding routing/resilience questions concretely, with your own grid topology and numbers
Quantify the trade-off between routing efficiency (cost/loss) and resilience (contingency robustness) for a specific recommendation
Produce a report a grid operator (or the AI Tutor evaluating this work) could actually act on
whyItMatters: This is the actual synthesis of Mission 5 and the direct answer to two of the very first concrete example questions the entire AI Energy OS mission was scoped around: minimizing routing cost, and surviving a multi-substation contingency. A mission that built every individual piece but never assembled them into one system that answers those founding questions concretely hasn't actually delivered on its own premise.
capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's complete grid-routing and contingency module — topology, routing, flow, resilience, and adaptive candidate selection combined into the system that directly answers the mission's founding questions with real numbers from your own topology.

problem.summary: Five separate pieces have never been assembled to answer the founding questions end to end.
problem.content: You've built topology modeling, shortest-path routing, flow analysis, resilience/contingency detection, and RL-augmented candidate selection as five separate pieces — but never actually assembled them to answer, end to end, on your own grid: 'what's the most efficient way to route power right now, and would it survive if two specific substations failed?'

motivation.summary: This is where the mission's founding questions get answered for real.
motivation.content: This is the actual moment this mission's founding questions get answered for real, with your own numbers, not as a hypothetical. 'How do I reduce energy costs by routing more efficiently' and 'how do I avoid overloading the grid if two substations fail' were never rhetorical — this lesson is where you produce the actual, checkable answer.

theory.summary: A complete report combines routing, resilience checking, and adaptive selection with an explicit trade-off.
theory.content: A complete grid-routing report combines: the current minimum-loss/minimum-cost routing recommendation (from shortest-path/flow analysis), an explicit resilience check confirming that recommendation would (or wouldn't) survive a specified contingency scenario, and — where conditions are dynamic enough to benefit from it — the RL-augmented candidate selection from the previous lesson choosing among several resilience-checked options rather than a single static best. Every recommendation should quantify its trade-off explicitly: a slightly more expensive route that survives a stated contingency versus a cheaper one that doesn't, mirroring the same honest-trade-off discipline established in Mission 4's neural-vs-classical verdict and Mission 5's RL explainability lesson — a recommendation without a stated trade-off isn't actually a complete answer.

visualExplanation.summary: Topology flows through routing/flow/resilience analysis and RL-augmented selection to one final recommendation.
visualExplanation.content: The full pipeline is shown: topology in, routing/flow/resilience analysis run in parallel, RL-augmented selection among resilience-checked candidates, converging on one final recommendation with an attached trade-off summary.

interactiveDemo.summary: Run the finished system against several live scenarios and inspect each recommendation.
interactiveDemo.content: Run the finished routing system against several live scenarios (normal conditions, a simulated two-substation contingency, a price spike) and inspect the generated recommendation and trade-off summary for each.

guidedCoding.summary: Follow along: assemble the full recommend_route pipeline with resilience filtering.

independentCoding.summary: Confirm the system falls back correctly when the cheapest route fails a contingency.
independentCoding.content: Run your complete routing system against a scenario where the lowest-loss route would NOT survive a specified two-substation contingency, and confirm the system correctly falls back to a resilience-checked alternative with a documented trade-off.

debugging.summary: Fix a system that never actually checks candidates against contingencies.
debugging.content: A system returns the raw lowest-loss candidate without ever actually checking it against the contingency scenarios, silently recommending a route that would fail if the specified substations went offline. Identify the missing resilience check and fix the recommendation logic.

reflection.summary: An optimal-looking but unchecked recommendation carries a real, hidden cost.
reflection.content: What's the real cost of a routing recommendation that looks optimal under normal conditions but was never actually checked against the contingency scenario the operator cares most about?

teachBack.summary: Synthesize Mission 5: walk through the full routing decision for a real scenario.
teachBack.content: As the synthesis of Mission 5 entirely: walk someone through your complete routing system's decision for a specific real scenario in your grid topology, from topology through routing, resilience, and final trade-off.

miniProject.summary: Deliver the final Mission 5 grid-routing and contingency report.
miniProject.content: Deliver the Digital Twin's final Mission 5 grid-routing and contingency report: for your own grid topology, the current best routing recommendation, an explicit two-substation contingency check, and a documented efficiency-vs-resilience trade-off a grid operator could act on.

portfolioEvidence.summary: This concretely answers the two founding questions the mission was scoped around.
portfolioEvidence.content: This report directly and concretely answers the two founding routing/resilience questions the entire AI Energy OS mission was scoped around — the tangible proof Mission 5 delivered on its own premise.

labs.title: Final System
labs.description: Sandboxed exercise assembling the complete routing/resilience/RL-augmented recommendation pipeline, requiring a fix for a planted missing-contingency-check bug and a correct fallback under a failing scenario.

simulations.title: Full Routing System Visualizer
simulations.description: End-to-end pipeline visualization from topology through routing, resilience checking, and RL-augmented selection to a final recommendation with trade-off summary.

flashcards.front: Why must a routing recommendation include an explicit trade-off statement, not just the chosen route?
flashcards.back: A recommendation without a stated trade-off (e.g. 'this route is slightly more expensive but survives the specified contingency') isn't actually a complete, checkable answer — the operator can't judge it without knowing what was traded against what.

flashcards.front: What should the system do if no candidate route survives all specified contingency scenarios?
flashcards.back: Report that explicitly — 'no candidate survives all specified contingencies' — rather than silently falling back to the best-looking option that doesn't actually satisfy the resilience requirement.

rubric: Final report answers both founding questions (routing efficiency and two-substation resilience) with concrete numbers from the learner's own topology
Every recommendation includes an explicit, quantified efficiency-vs-resilience trade-off
System correctly falls back to a resilience-checked alternative when the naive lowest-loss route fails a contingency
